pages.text: produce a reduction of workability. The compressive strength was 20% lower than that of conventional concrete, tensile splitting strength and modulus elasticity were similar to those in conven- tional concrete. Siddique [9,10], suggested that use of used-foun- dry sand (UFS) could be conveniently used in making good quality concrete and construction materials. Over recent decades, the steelmaking industry in Spain has been transformed, as electric arc furnaces have largely replaced blast furnaces, leading to the appearance of a new by-product: electric arc furnace slag (black slag, EAFS). 1.7 M tons of this type of slag are produced annually in Spain. During the last years several re- search works have been carried out into the use of EAFS on bitumi- nous mixes [11], granulate bases for roads [12,13], cement production [14], as aggregates for concrete production [15,16] and some others uses [17,18]. The possibility of EAFS slag being used satisfactorily in concrete has been demonstrated [19,20]. EAFS slag concrete has good mechanical properties and its high density is an advantageous property when weight of concrete is a key factor. As blast furnace slag concretes are concerned, their reuse is now well known. They are often reused as a component of cement and as aggregate in road construction or in concretes [21]. Its use as aggregate in concrete is not very usual although it has no behav- ioural problems, however there has been little research work done on the subject. In this research study, two types of concretes were produced using used-foundry sand as substitution for raw ﬁne aggregates: concrete made with chemical foundry sand (C-QFS) and concrete made with green foundry sand (C-GFS). EAFS and BFS were used as substitution for coarse raw aggregates in 25%, 50% and 100% by volume, in order to produce C-EAFS25, C-EAFS50, C-EAFS100, C-BFS25, C-BFS50 and C-BFS100 concretes. Two concrete produc- tion stages were carried out, stages 1 and 2, with high water–ce- ment ratio and medium water–cement ratio, respectively. In stage 1, slump test and hardened concrete properties of compres- sive strength, tensile splitting strength and modulus of elasticity at 28 d of curing were determined. In stage 2, due to the adequate properties of all concretes, slump test, hardened properties of den- sity, compressive strength, tensile splitting strength, modulus elas- ticity, length change (during 56 weeks), sorptivity and high temperature exposure evaluation at 28 d of curing were deter- mined. The tests results obtained from concretes produced in stages 1 and 2 were compared with those in conventional concrete (CC). 2. Materials and experimental details 2.1. Materials 2.1.1. Cement CEM I 52.5R, a high quality, high strength rapid-hardening Portland cement was used in all concretes. Its density was 3.05 kg/cm 3 and its chemical analysis and con- stituent are illustrated in Tables 1 and 2 , respectively. 2.1.2. Metallurgical sub-products Three sub-products were used for concrete production: used-foundry sand (green foundry sand (GFS) and chemically bonded foundry sand (QFS)), electric arc furnace slag (EAFS) and blast furnace slag (BFS). 2.1.2.1. Physical properties. The UFS were employed as partial replacement of ﬁne aggregate (raw sand) according to grading size of the UFS. The fraction of UFS-s materials were deﬁned by 0/0.5 mm sieves. In order to use UFS for concrete produc- tion the natural sand, deﬁned by 0/4 mm fraction was divided into two fract

absorption capacity was lower than 5% (the maximum absorption capacity require- ment according to Spanish concrete structural code, EHE-2008). EAFS has the higher density and lower absorption capacity than BFS. 2.1.2.2. Chemical properties. The chemical composition of QFS, GFS, EAFS and BFS, determined by X-ray ﬂuorescence, is given in Table 4 . Alkali silica reactivity was determined for all by-products; this test was practiced according to UNE 146508:1999 EX. The expansion suffered by QFS, GFS, EAFS and BFS at 14 d were lower than 0.1%, consequently they were considered none expansive materials. The leaching test according BS EN 12457-2:2002 was carried out for all by- products materials and according to minimum values required for waste materials the by-product QFS, GFS, EAFS and BS were classiﬁed as inert material. 2.1.3. Raw aggregates Three fractions of limestone raw aggregates were used for concrete production: sand (NS: 0/4 mm), coarse aggregates (CA1: 4/10 mm) and coarse aggregates (CA2: 10/20 mm). The grading of the raw materials were determined according to UNE- EN 933-1:1998 (see Fig. 3). Table 5 shows the physical properties of raw aggregates, they were determined according to BS EN 1097-6:2000. 2.1.4. Additive In order to achieve the same workability in all concretes, a super plasticizer composed by vinylic modiﬁed copolymers and organic mineral agents was used. Its relative density (20 /C176C) was 1.15 kg/l and the recommended dosage was between 0.5% and 2% of the cement weight. 2.2. Experimental details 2.2.1. Concrete mix proportions The Bolomey dosage method [22,23] was used in the mixing of all concretes. The mechanical properties of concretes produced using used-foundry sand (C-QFS and C-GFS), electrical arc furnace slag (C-EAFS) and blast furnace slag (C-BFS) were compared with those of conventional concrete (CC). The conventional concrete (CC) was produced using three fractions of aggre- gates, NS (0/4 mm), CA1 (4/10 mm), CA2 (10/20 mm). The mix proportions of the two concretes made with UFS (concrete made with chemical (C-QFS) and green foundry sand(C-GFS)) were produced using four fractions of aggregates: UFS (0/ 0,5 mm), fractioned natural sand (FNS, 0.5/4 mm), CA1 (4/10 mm) and CA2 (10/ 20 mm). The concretes produced with EAFS and BFS were manufactured using NS (0/4 mm), raw coarse aggregates being substituted by 25%, 50% and 100% by volume EAFS and BFS. They are C-EAFS25, C-EAF50, C-EAF100, C-BFS25, C-BFS50 and C- BFS100, respectively. In order to deﬁne the mix proportions of the concretes, the cement amount per m 3 of concrete and effective water–cement ratio were ﬁxed. Due to the difference between the water absorption capacity of by-products, the same effective w/c ratio was deﬁned in all concrete mixes in order to detect the inﬂuence of the aggregates on the concrete properties. In order to calculate the effective water–cement ratio, the water absorption capacity of the aggregates after 10 min immersion in water (deﬁned by effective absorption capacity) was determined and was added at the moment of concrete production in order to maintain the same effective water–ce- ment ratio in all concretes. The total w/c ratio depends on the humidity (it was measured before concrete production) and effective absorption capacity (absorption capacity on 10 min) of the aggregates. The QFS and GFS absorbed the same amount of water at 10 min and 24 h. The water absorption capacity of EAFS and BFS absorbed at 10 min was 1.0

specimen. The lower areas on the sides of the specimens were coated with grease to achieve unidirectional ﬂow. The cumulative water absorbed was recorded at differ- ent time intervals of up to 2 h by weighing the specimen after removing the surface water using a dampened tissue. In order to determine high temperature exposure behavior of concretes, after 28 d of curing, the specimens of concretes produces in stage 2 were heated in an electric furnace up to 800 /C176C during 4 h. The specimens were allowed to cool natu- rally to room temperature, they were tested for compression and their strength was compared to those which had not been exposed to high temperatures. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Fresh concrete properties Table 8 shows the results of the slump test for all concretes. Minimum amount of additive was employed in all concrete pro- duction except in C-EAFS100 concrete mix stage 1 (see Table 6 ). The additive was not employed in the manufacture of the conven- tional concrete (CC) and concretes made with used-foundry sand (C-QFS and C-GFS) in stage 2, and they obtained adequate work- ability. Concretes produced with low percentages of EAFS and BFS obtained similar slump test value to CC. However, the high amount of slag and above all the high amount of EAFS in concrete production considerably reduced the workability of concrete. In both stages it has been veriﬁed that concretes produced with chemical foundry sand obtained the most workable concrete. 3.2. Hardened concrete properties 3.2.1. Density and mechanical properties Tables 9 and 10 illustrate the hardened concrete properties of the concretes produced in stages 1 and 2, respectively. Each of the presented values is the average of three measurements. Con- cretes produced in stage 1 obtained low values of hardened prop- erties due to the use of a high water–cement ratio (the used w/c ratio was higher than 0.65 in all concretes except in C-QFS). Con- cretes made with used-foundry sand obtained the most adequate properties. Those two concretes obtained higher compressive strength than 25 MPa. C-QFS and C-GFS obtained a higher com- pressive and splitting tensile strength than the conventional con- cretes as shown in Fig. 4 . Concretes made with EAFS obtained the highest hardened prop- erties in stage 2. Due to the lower free water amount in stage 2, CC and concretes made with BFS (C-BFS25, C-BFS50 and C-BFS100) ob- tained adequate hardened properties. When higher percentages of EAFS and BFS were used in concrete production higher compres- sive strength was obtained. Concretes produced with EAFS and BFS obtained a higher compressive strength than CC (see Fig. 5 ). Conventional concrete obtained the highest splitting tensile strength and the concretes made with used-foundry sands ob- tained the lowest hardened properties, C-GFS being the weakest concrete (see Fig. 5 ). According to the density, the concretes produced with EAFS were the densest and concretes made with BFS were the lightest due to the high and low density of by-products, respectively. Figs. 6 and 7 describe the development over time (from 7 d to 3 months) of the compressive strengths of concretes produced in Table 6 Concrete mix proportions for stage 1. stages 1 and 2, respectively. Due to high water–cement ratio in concretes produced in stage 1, as shown Fig. 6 there is not a clear tendency of compressive strength development from 7 d to 3 months of curing. The concretes produced with EAFS and BFS ob- tained the lowest compressive strength after 3 months of curing. With time all concretes produced in stage 2 suffered similar development of compressive strength. The concretes produced with EAFS and BFS also obtained the highest strength after 3 months of curing and the concretes produced with used-foundry sand did not reach the strength of conventional concrete. 3.2.2. Length change, sorptivity and exposure to high temperature of stage 2 hardened concrete As shown in Fig. 8, concrete specimens made with industrial by- products suffered similar length change to those of conventional concrete after they all were submerged in water from 12 weeks to 56 weeks. All the concretes suffered a slight length change after being submerged for 12 weeks, after which time the specimen length of the concretes maintained constant. CC suffered the high- est length variation, approximately 1.5% during 12 weeks (ﬁrst measurement was taken after 24 h of casting). From 12 to 56 weeks submerged in water the length of the specimens main- tained constant. According to the sorptivity test made on concretes produced in stage 2, conventional concrete was the most absorbent material, after C-EAFS25 and EAFS50. However, the sorption of the concretes after 120 min was low and did not decrease as shown in Fig. 9 . Consequently it was veriﬁed that the concretes made with by- products transmit low water by capillarity. As shown in Table 11 , CC obtained the lowest compressive strength after exposure to high temperatures, followed by the Table 10 Hardened concrete properties of stage 2 at 28 d of curing. C-QFS. The concretes made with EAFS and BFS suffered the lowest reduction of compressive strength after high temperature expo- sure and they maintained 50% of their initial compressive strength. CC and C-QFS reduced 60% of their compressive strength after high temperature exposure. 4. Conclusions In accordance with the experimental phase carried out in this study, the conclusions obtained are: – Concrete made with chemical foundry sand obtains the highest workability, and the use of high percentages (>50%) of slag as coarse aggregates considerably reduces the workability of the concrete. – Concretes made with green or chemical foundry sand obtain more adequate mechanical properties than conventional concrete when the concrete is produced with high water– cement ratio. – Concretes produce with electric arc furnace slag and blast furnace slag as replacement for raw coarse aggregates obtain a higher compressive strength than the conven- tional concrete when the concrete is made with a medium Fig. 6. Compressive strength development of concretes in stage 1. Fig. 7. Compressive strength development of concrete in stage 2. Fig. 8. Length change of hardened concretes during 56 weeks. Fig. 9. The sorptivity of concretes made with different by-products as aggregates versus time. The specimens were oven dried until constant weight, after 28 d of curing. water–cement ratio. The splitting tensile strength and modulus of elasticity are similar to conventional concrete. C-GFS being the weakest concrete according to mechanical properties. – Concretes produced with metallurgical industrial by-prod- ucts suffered similar length change to that of conventional concrete. – It is veriﬁed that the concretes made with metallurgical by- products transmit lower water by capillarity and obtain higher residual compressive strength after high tempera- ture exposure to those of conventional concrete. Properties of concrete using metallurgical industrial by-products as aggregates M. Etxeberria a,*, C. Pacheco b, J.M. Meneses b, I. Berridi a a Department of Construction Engineering, Polytechnic University of Catalonia, Spain b Instituto Tecnológico De Rocas Ornamentales Y Materiales De Construcción (INTROMAC), Campus University of Extremadura, Cáceres, Spain article info Article history: Received 3 September 2009 Received in revised form 15 February 2010 Accepted 18 February 2010 Available online 17 March 2010 Keywords: Metallurgical industrial by-products Used-foundry sand Electric arc furnace slag Blast furnace slag Aggregates Concrete Workability Concrete hardened properties abstract The use of industrial by-products and waste materials in concrete opens a whole new range of possibil- ities in the reuse of materials in the building industry. In this research study, concretes were made with chemical foundry sand (QFS) and green foundry sand (GFS) as substitution for raw sand. Also Electric arc furnace slag (EAFS) and blast furnace slag (BSF) were used as substitution for coarse raw aggregates in 25%, 50% and 100% of concrete production. Two concrete production stages were carried out. In stages 1 and 2, slump test and compressive and tensile splitting strengths and modulus of elasticity were deter- mined. Due to adequate properties of all concretes found in stage 2, length change (during 56 weeks), sorptivity and high temperature exposure evaluation were also determined. The tests results obtained from concretes produced in stages 1 and 2 were compared with those of conventional concrete (CC) and the adequate use of the by-products for concrete production was veriﬁed. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction As the industrial production of metals increases, the amount of wastes generated becomes an environmental problem that must be dealt with. It is imperative for the future that these materials are regarded as resources to be utilized in other industries. The use of by-products in concrete has hitherto been limited in Spain. One reason is that there has always been a large supply of natural raw materials available. Another reason is that the concrete pro- ducers prefer to use well known materials instead of new ones, since new materials have to be evaluated in terms of mechanical properties, durability and service life before they can be approved for construction. The purposes of this investigation have been to show that by-products can be used in concrete as aggregates. Foundry sand (FS), electric arc furnace slag (EAFS) and blast fur- nace slag (BFS), by-products of ferrous and nonferrous metal pro- duction and casting industries were used as aggregates for concrete production in this research work. Sand has been used for centuries as a molding material because of its thermal conductivity. Foundries successfully recycle and re- use the sand many times in foundry production. When the sand can no longer be reused in the foundry, it is removed from the foundry and is termed foundry sand. During 2006 Spain [1] pro- duced more than a million tons of these materials, 70% of foundry sand is discarded annually and is available to be recycled into other products and is used in other industries. Foundries purchase high quality size-speciﬁc silica sands for use in their molding and cast- ing operations. The classiﬁcation of foundry sand depends on the type of binder system used in metal casting. Usually, two types of bi
